section.title: Instructions for the Priests
section.sectionRationale: Having given the offerings from the worshiper's side, the LORD now turns to the priests themselves, the torah, the law or instruction, for how they are to handle the burnt, grain, and sin offerings, including the perpetual fire and the eating of most holy portions.
section.thesis: God gives Aaron and his sons detailed instructions for maintaining the altar's continual fire and for handling the burnt, grain, and sin offerings, teaching that the priests who mediate atonement must themselves treat every holy thing with exacting care.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the law of the burnt offering and its perpetual fire; the law of the grain offering, eaten by the priests in a holy place; and the law of the sin offering, most holy, requiring careful handling of its blood.

units.label: The perpetual fire
units.note: the law of the burnt offering

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Moses,

“Command Aaron and his sons that this is the {{torah|law}} of the burnt offering: The burnt offering is to remain on the hearth of the altar all night, until morning, and the {{tamid|fire must be kept burning}} on the altar.
And the priest shall put on his linen robe and linen undergarments, and he shall remove from the altar the ashes of the burnt offering that the fire has consumed and place them beside it.
Then he must take off his garments, put on other clothes, and carry the ashes outside the camp to a ceremonially clean place.

The fire on the altar shall be kept burning; it must not be extinguished. Every morning the priest is to add wood to the fire, arrange the burnt offering on it, and burn the fat portions of the peace offerings on it.
The fire shall be kept burning on the altar continually; it must not be extinguished.

units.label: The law of the grain offering
units.note: eaten in a holy place, most holy

verses.text: Now this is the law of the grain offering: Aaron's sons shall present it before the LORD in front of the altar.
The priest is to remove a handful of fine flour and olive oil, together with all the frankincense from the grain offering, and burn the memorial portion on the altar as a pleasing aroma to the LORD.
Aaron and his sons are to eat the remainder. It must be eaten without leaven in a holy place; they are to eat it in the courtyard of the Tent of Meeting.
It must not be baked with leaven; I have assigned it as their portion of My offerings made by fire. It is {{qodeshQodashim|most holy}}, like the sin offering and the guilt offering.
Any male among the sons of Aaron may eat it. This is a permanent portion from the offerings made by fire to the LORD for the generations to come. Anything that touches them will become holy.”
Then the LORD said to Moses,

“This is the offering that Aaron and his sons must present to the LORD on the day he is anointed: a tenth of an ephah of fine flour as a regular grain offering, half of it in the morning and half in the evening.
It shall be prepared with oil on a griddle; you are to bring it well-kneaded and present it as a grain offering broken in pieces, a pleasing aroma to the LORD.
The priest, who is one of Aaron's sons and will be anointed to take his place, is to prepare it. As a permanent portion for the LORD, it must be burned completely.
Every grain offering for a priest shall be burned completely; it is not to be eaten.”

units.label: The law of the sin offering

verses.text: And the LORD said to Moses,
“Tell Aaron and his sons that this is the law of the sin offering: In the place where the burnt offering is slaughtered, the sin offering shall be slaughtered before the LORD; it is most holy.
The priest who offers it shall eat it; it must be eaten in a holy place, in the courtyard of the Tent of Meeting.
Anything that touches its flesh will become holy, and if any of the blood is spattered on a garment, you must wash it in a holy place.
The clay pot in which the sin offering is boiled must be broken; if it is boiled in a bronze pot, the pot must be scoured and rinsed with water.
Any male among the priests may eat it; it is most holy.
But no sin offering may be eaten if its blood has been brought into the Tent of Meeting to make atonement in the Holy Place; it must be burned.

torah.gloss: Law or instruction, from a root meaning to teach or point the way. Each offering in this chapter has its own torah, its own detailed instruction, showing that God's law is fundamentally a patient teaching of how to live and worship rightly, not a bare list of rules.

tamid.gloss: Continually, perpetually, without ceasing. The altar fire that must never go out, tamid, pictures unceasing access to atonement and worship, a fire that must be tended faithfully by the priests day and night.

qodeshQodashim.gloss: Most holy, the highest level of consecration. The grain and sin offerings eaten by the priests are qodesh qodashim, so holy that anything touching them becomes holy too, requiring careful handling and eating only in a sacred place.

basic.context: Having given instructions for worshipers bringing offerings, God now turns to the priests themselves, giving them the torah, the detailed instruction, for handling the burnt, grain, and sin offerings. The altar fire must never go out, the grain offering is eaten by the priests in a holy place, and the sin offering, most holy, requires such careful handling that even the pot it is cooked in must be broken or scoured.
basic.hermeneutics: This describes actual procedural instructions for Israel's old covenant priesthood, not a pattern for how Christian ministers today must handle sacred objects. Its abiding truth is that those who mediate access to God bear a serious responsibility to treat what is holy with exacting, careful reverence.
basic.originalLanguages: Each offering has its own torah, instruction, from a root meaning to teach, showing that law in Scripture is fundamentally patient guidance, not arbitrary restriction. The altar fire burns tamid, continually, and the priests' portions of the grain and sin offerings are qodesh qodashim, most holy, so sacred that contact with them makes ordinary things holy too.
basic.typology: The fire that must never go out pictures the unceasing, always available access to atonement that God provided for Israel, anticipating the permanently open access believers now have through Christ's finished work. The priests eating what is most holy, bearing the weight of the people's guilt as they consume the sin offering, foreshadows Christ, who bore the sin of His people in His own body.
basic.christ: Just as the priests bore away the guilt of the people by eating the sin offering, so Christ, our great High Priest, bore our sins in His own body on the tree, 1 Peter 2:24. The unceasing fire on the altar anticipates the once for all, never repeated sacrifice of Christ, whose atonement never needs to be relit or renewed.
basic.god: God cares intensely about the details of how His holy things are handled, not because He is arbitrary, but because holiness itself is contagious in this system, spreading to whatever touches it, and profaned by carelessness. He is a God who takes the mediating work of His priests with the utmost seriousness.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Peter 2:24 says Christ bore our sins in His own body on the tree, echoing the priest who bore the sin offering by eating it. Hebrews 10:11-14 contrasts the priests who stood daily, offering repeated sacrifices, with Christ, who sat down after one sacrifice for sins forever. Hebrews 7:27 says Christ has no need to offer sacrifices daily as those priests did.
basic.oneStory: This chapter shows God instructing His priests to treat every holy thing with exacting care, since holiness is not casual and guilt is not lightly handled. It anticipates Christ, our great High Priest, who bore our sin fully in His own body, and whose single sacrifice means the fire of atonement, once lit at the cross, never needs to be relit again.

context.literary: This chapter turns from the worshiper's perspective, addressed in chapters one through five, to the priest's perspective, restating the burnt, grain, and sin offerings from the angle of who tends the altar and who eats what portions.
context.historical: Priests in the ancient world often handled offerings with great ritual complexity; Israel's priesthood was distinguished by the moral and covenantal seriousness attached to every detail, teaching that access to God could never be treated casually.
context.canonical: The unceasing altar fire and the priestly consumption of most holy offerings become vocabulary the New Testament draws on to describe both Christ's once for all sacrifice and believers' call to treat what is holy with reverence, compare Hebrews 12:28-29.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To instruct Aaron and his sons in the careful, ongoing procedures required to maintain the altar and rightly handle the most holy portions of the burnt, grain, and sin offerings, so that the priesthood's mediating work would never become careless or casual.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This prescribes specific procedures for Israel's old covenant priesthood; it is not a manual for church practice today. Its abiding principle is that those who handle what is sacred, whether priests then or ministers now, bear real responsibility to treat holy things with reverence and care.

debates.question: Why must a clay pot used to boil the sin offering be broken, while a bronze pot need only be scoured, verse 28?

views.label: Porous versus non porous materials
views.case: Clay is porous and can absorb residue from the most holy offering in a way that cannot be fully cleaned, so it must be destroyed, while bronze, being non porous, can be adequately scoured and reused.

views.label: A picture of holiness that cannot be casually reused
views.case: Others see the broken pot as a vivid, tangible lesson that contact with what is most holy permanently changes an object, a symbol reinforcing the seriousness of handling sacred things even beyond a merely practical concern.

debates.bearing: Either reading underscores the same point, that the most holy offerings required a level of care matched to their sacredness, whatever the practical or symbolic reason for the specific rule.

study.originalLanguages: Each offering carries its own torah, teaching or instruction, showing that God's law patiently guides rather than merely restricts. The altar fire must burn tamid, continually, day and night, and the priestly portions of the grain and sin offerings are qodesh qodashim, most holy, so sacred that contact with them renders ordinary objects holy as well.

typology.type: The fire that must never go out
typology.fulfillment: The unceasing, permanently secured access to atonement won once for all by Christ, who never needs to offer again, Hebrews 7:27.

typology.type: The priest eating the sin offering, bearing the people's guilt
typology.fulfillment: Christ, our great High Priest, who bore our sins in His own body on the tree, 1 Peter 2:24.

study.christ: Hebrews 10:11-14 draws a sharp contrast between priests who stood daily, offering the same sacrifices repeatedly, and Christ, who offered one sacrifice for sins and sat down, His work finished. Where Aaron's sons had to tend an unceasing fire and repeat the offerings endlessly, Christ's single sacrifice needs no relighting, no repetition, and no ongoing maintenance; it is complete.
study.god: God's insistence on exacting care in these instructions, from the fire that must never go out to the pot that must be broken, reveals how seriously He regards the handling of holy things. He is not distant from the details of worship but deeply invested in how His priests carry out even the smallest procedures of atonement.

crossReferences.note: Christ bore our sins in His own body on the tree, echoing the priest who bore the sin offering's guilt by eating it in a holy place.
Priests stood daily offering repeated sacrifices, but Christ offered one sacrifice for sins and sat down at God's right hand.
Unlike those priests, Christ has no need to offer sacrifices daily, since He offered Himself once for all.

study.oneStory: This chapter shows a God who requires His priests to handle every holy thing, from the altar's unceasing fire to the sin offering's most holy portions, with exacting reverence. It anticipates Christ, the great High Priest who bore our sin fully in Himself and whose once for all sacrifice means the fire of atonement never needs to be relit again.

quiz.q: What must never happen to the fire on the altar, according to this chapter?

options.text: It must be kept burning continually and never extinguished.
options.note: Verses 9, 12, and 13 all repeat this requirement for a perpetual, unceasing fire.

options.text: It must be put out every evening and relit each morning.
options.note: The text explicitly requires the opposite, a fire that continues burning without interruption.

options.text: It must be relit once a year at Passover.
options.note: The instruction concerns a continual fire, not an annual relighting ritual.

options.text: No instructions are given about the fire.
options.note: Verses 9 to 13 give detailed instructions specifically about maintaining the fire.

quiz.q: Where must Aaron and his sons eat the remainder of the grain offering?

options.text: In a holy place, the courtyard of the Tent of Meeting.
options.note: Verse 16 specifies this exact location for eating the grain offering.

options.text: In their own homes, wherever they lived.
options.note: The text requires it be eaten in a holy place, not in ordinary homes.

options.text: Anywhere in the wilderness camp.
options.note: The requirement is specifically the courtyard of the Tent of Meeting, a holy place, not anywhere in the camp.

options.text: No location is specified.
options.note: Verse 16 explicitly specifies the location.

quiz.q: What must be done with a clay pot in which the sin offering has been boiled?

options.text: It must be broken.
options.note: Verse 28 explicitly requires breaking the clay pot, while a bronze pot need only be scoured and rinsed.

options.text: It must be kept permanently in the sanctuary.
options.note: The text requires the pot be broken, not preserved.

options.text: It must be given to the worshiper who brought the offering.
options.note: The pot's disposal is prescribed as broken; it is not given away to the worshiper.

options.text: Nothing needs to be done with it.
options.note: Verse 28 gives a specific requirement for the clay pot.

quiz.q: What special grain offering must Aaron present on the day of his anointing, and every day after as the ongoing priestly portion?

options.text: A tenth of an ephah of fine flour, half offered in the morning and half in the evening, burned completely.
options.note: Verses 20 to 23 describe this offering and specify it must be burned completely, unlike ordinary grain offerings that priests eat.

options.text: A whole bull, burned entirely each morning.
options.note: The offering specified is a grain offering of fine flour, not a bull.

options.text: Nothing additional beyond the regular grain offering.
options.note: Verses 19 to 23 describe a specific, additional offering unique to the anointed priest.

options.text: No such offering is described.
options.note: Verses 19 to 23 give a detailed description of this offering.

quiz.q: When must a sin offering be burned rather than eaten by the priests, according to verse 30?

options.text: When its blood has been brought into the Tent of Meeting to make atonement in the Holy Place.
options.note: Verse 30 gives this exact condition, matching the procedure described earlier in chapter 4 for the priest's and congregation's sin offerings.

options.text: Whenever the offering is brought by a leader rather than a common person.
options.note: The distinguishing factor named in verse 30 is whether the blood entered the Tent of Meeting, not the rank of the one who sinned.

options.text: Only on the Sabbath day.
options.note: No such Sabbath restriction is mentioned in this verse.

options.text: No exception to eating the sin offering is given.
options.note: Verse 30 explicitly states this exception.

questions: The altar fire had to be tended continually, never allowed to go out. What spiritual disciplines in your own life need that same kind of continual, faithful tending rather than occasional attention?

The priests had to change clothes before carrying out the ashes, treating even that task with careful reverence. Where in your own service to God have you become careless about details that actually matter?

The most holy portions could only be eaten in a designated holy place, never casually. Do you treat your access to God, through prayer and His word, with the seriousness it deserves, or has it become routine?

The priest who ate the sin offering bore the weight of the people's guilt in that act. How does it change your view of Christ to know He literally bore your sin in His own body, not just symbolically?

Christ's sacrifice was offered once, and He sat down, His work finished, unlike the priests who stood daily repeating the ritual. Are you still striving to earn what Christ has already completed for you?

translationNotes.note: The fire must be kept burning, using the Hebrew tamid, continually, is the same word used elsewhere for the daily, regular offerings, tying together the ideas of unceasing fire and unceasing worship.

Anything that touches them will become holy describes a principle of contagious holiness in this ritual system, where contact with what is most holy conveys a derived sanctity requiring careful handling.

The distinction between breaking a clay pot and scouring a bronze one reflects the different porosity of the materials; clay could retain residue from the most holy offering in a way bronze would not.
